did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study utilized an Artificial Intelligence as a Medical Device (AIaMD) built on convolutional neural networks (specifically a deep ensemble for the recognition of malignancy, DERM) to analyze dermoscopic images of skin lesions. The AI was used to classify lesions as malignant (melanoma, SCC, BCC) or benign/premalignant (IEC, AK, AN) and to determine referral needs, with its performance compared against teledermatology standard of care in a UK-based skin cancer teledermatology service.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as 'TYPE Original Research' and describes a 'prospective, single-centre, single-arm, masked, non-inferiority, adaptive, group sequential design trial' (NCT04123678). It reports original experimental data from 700 patients with 867 lesions, including methods, results (sensitivity, specificity, p-values), and discussion of new findings regarding an AIaMD's accuracy compared to standard of care.

title: Accuracy of an artificial intelligence as a medical device as part of a UK-based skin cancer teledermatology service
brief_summary: This prospective, single-centre trial evaluated the accuracy of an Artificial Intelligence as a Medical Device (AIaMD), specifically the Deep Ensemble for the Recognition of Malignancy (DERM), within a UK-based skin cancer teledermatology service. The study recruited 700 patients with 867 suspicious skin lesions, which were assessed by both the AIaMD and a consultant dermatologist, with outcomes compared against final histological or clinical diagnoses. The primary objective was to determine if the AIaMD could correctly classify more lesions as not requiring biopsy or urgent referral compared to the teledermatology standard of care while maintaining equivalent sensitivity for malignancy. Results showed that the AIaMD achieved a significantly higher rate of identifying lesions that did not need urgent referral or biopsy compared to the standard of care (p=0.001), with comparable sensitivity for detecting skin cancer. The study concludes that integrating this AIaMD into teledermatology services has the potential to reduce the burden of unnecessary referrals.

lesion_types_studied.details: Histology-confirmed melanoma cases were used as a primary outcome. 8 histology-diagnosed melanomas were identified in the study population. The AIaMD and SoC sensitivity for melanoma was a key metric.
Histology-confirmed SCC cases were used as a primary outcome. 13 histology-confirmed SCCs were identified. The AIaMD and SoC sensitivity for SCC was a key metric.
Histology-confirmed BCC cases were used as a primary outcome. 46 histology-confirmed BCCs were identified. The AIaMD and SoC sensitivity for BCC was a key metric.
Grouped with AK and AN for specificity analysis of premalignant/benign lesions. Specificity was defined as the percentage of lesions diagnosed as IEC, AK, AN, or benign that were correctly labeled.
Grouped with IEC and AN for specificity analysis of premalignant/benign lesions. Number needed to refer for IEC and AK was calculated in post-hoc analysis.
Grouped with IEC and AK for specificity analysis of premalignant/benign lesions.

Grouped with IEC, AK, and AN for specificity analysis. The AIaMD outputs benign labels for individual benign conditions which are grouped into one output. Specificity was defined as the percentage of lesions diagnosed as IEC, AK, AN, or benign that were correctly labeled.
Table 1 lists 'Other' (43 lesions, 6.9%) and 'Unknown' (11 lesions, 1.8%) as diagnostic categories in the study population, though specific entities are not detailed.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions (Melanoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Intraepidermal Carcinoma/Bowen's Disease, Actinic Keratosis, Atypical Nevus, or Benign).
all_task_types.details: The AIaMD (DERM) classifies dermoscopic images into specific malignant, premalignant, and benign diagnostic categories. The study evaluates sensitivity, specificity, and predictive values for these discrete classes. The primary objective was to classify lesions as not needing referral (benign/premalignant) versus needing biopsy/urgent referral (malignant), comparing the AI's classification rate against the standard of care.

datasets_used.name: Impact Study Cohort (NCT04123678)
datasets_used.description: A prospective, single-centre, single-arm, masked, non-inferiority, adaptive, group sequential design trial conducted at Chelsea and Westminster NHS Foundation Trust between February 2020 and August 2021. It recruited patients referred to a skin cancer teledermatology cancer pathway. The dataset consists of dermoscopic images captured via iPhone XR smartphone with DermLite DL1 basic dermoscopic lens attachment by healthcare assistants, alongside clinical data and final histological or clinical diagnoses.

use.testing: The dataset was used as the primary evaluation cohort to assess the accuracy of the AIaMD (DERM) against the teledermatology standard of care (SoC) and final histological/clinical diagnoses. It served as the test set for the clinical trial.
use.other: The study mentions that the AIaMD algorithm was previously trained on dermoscopic images of skin lesions from multiple sources, but the specific training datasets are not named or detailed in this paper. This study's cohort was used for external validation/clinical evaluation of the pre-existing AIaMD.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To report statistical estimates of accuracy (sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, FNR, FPR, NNB, NNR) for the AIaMD and standard of care.
To analyze the primary endpoint (rate of correctly classifying lesions that did not need biopsy or urgent referral) in the interim analysis of phase 1.
To combine p-values from both phases of the adaptive group sequential design trial for the final analysis.
To adjust the one-sided significance level (alpha) for the final analysis based on the group sequential design.

statistical_methods_used.method: Sample size calculation based on non-inferiority margin
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To determine the required sample size (634 lesions) to demonstrate that the AIaMD specificity was not inferior to the standard of care using a 1% non-inferiority margin and 99% power.
To evaluate diagnostic accuracy indices (sensitivity, specificity) using real-world deployment threshold settings (AIaMD-RWS) for comparative purposes.

hypothesis_testing.test: One-sided, 2-proportion Z-test (for interim analysis); Lehmacher and Wassmer procedure for combining p-values from both phases (for final analysis)
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: The AIaMD's rate of correctly classifying lesions that did not need biopsy or urgent referral is not higher than the teledermatology standard of care (SoC).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: The AIaMD's rate of correctly classifying lesions that did not need biopsy or urgent referral is higher than the teledermatology standard of care (SoC).
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The AIaMD identified a significantly higher rate of lesions that did not need a biopsy or urgent referral compared to SoC (p-value < 0.0246 for the final analysis, which was adjusted from the overall alpha of 0.05 using the O’Brien-Fleming approach). The interim analysis also confirmed non-futility.

Impact Study Cohort (NCT04123678) - Per-.metric: Comparison of Standard of Care (SoC) vs AIaMD (Settings A and B) vs AIaMD-RWS
Impact Study Cohort (NCT04123678) - Per-.notes: The AIaMD identified a significantly higher rate of lesions that did not need a biopsy or urgent referral compared to SoC (p-value = 0.001 for Settings A/B; p-value < 0.0246 for final analysis). Sensitivity for skin cancer was comparable between AIaMD and SoC. In the RWS analysis, the AIaMD correctly identified all 8 histology-diagnosed melanomas, 11 of 13 SCCs, and 21 of 46 BCCs.

architecture_used.main_model: Deep Ensemble for the Recognition of Malignancy (DERM)
architecture_used.pretraining: Trained on dermoscopic images of skin lesions from multiple sources; previously demonstrated high sensitivity for melanoma in prior studies (e.g., DERM-003 study). Specific pretraining datasets or base model architectures (e.g., ResNet, EfficientNet) are not detailed in this paper.
architecture_used.input_size: Dermoscopic images captured via iPhone XR smartphone with DermLite DL1 basic dermoscopic lens attachment. Specific pixel dimensions are not reported.

classification_or_output_head.thresholds: Sensitivity thresholds were set at 91% and 92.5% for study settings (A and B) to match literature-defined clinician sensitivity. Real-world settings (RWS) used thresholds >95% for Melanoma/SCC and >90% for BCC.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Deep Ensemble for the Recognition of Malignancy (DERM)
Artificial Intelligence as a Medical Device (AIaMD)
preprocessing_techniques: Image quality assessment/check to exclude lesions with missing dermoscopic images or failed quality checks
Standardization of input devices (iPhone XR smartphone with DermLite DL1 basic dermoscopic lens attachment) to ensure consistent input for the AI model
conclusions: The Artificial Intelligence as a Medical Device (AIaMD) identified a significantly higher rate of lesions that did not need to be referred for biopsy or urgent face-to-face dermatologist review compared to the teledermatology standard of care (SoC) (p-value = 0.001).
The AIaMD achieved comparable sensitivity for detecting skin cancer (malignancy) to the teledermatology standard of care.
Integrating this AIaMD into teledermatology services has the potential to reduce the burden of unnecessary referrals.

The study utilized a specific smartphone and dermatoscope combination (iPhone XR with DermLite DL1), which differs from previous studies using DSLR cameras, limiting direct performance comparisons across different device types.
The results of this specific AIaMD cannot be generalized to other AI-based skin cancer detection tools, as performance varies significantly among different systems.

The AIaMD evaluated is a Class IIa UKCA Medical Device, providing verification of safety from the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) and supporting its use for triaging patients to improve access and early diagnosis.
important_remarks: Single-centre design: The study was conducted at a single site (Chelsea and Westminster NHS Foundation Trust) in North West London, limiting generalizability to other regions or healthcare systems.

Demographic bias: The cohort was predominantly white (89.0%) with a median age of 51; only 4.0% of participants had Fitzpatrick skin types IV-VI, making it insufficient to evaluate AI performance in darker skin types.

Device specificity: Only one smartphone (iPhone XR) and dermatoscope (DermLite DL1) combination was used; results cannot be directly compared to studies using DSLR cameras or other devices without qualification.
Low incidence of rare cancers: No rare skin cancers (e.g., Merkel cell carcinoma) were identified due to low incidence in the study population, limiting evidence for detection of these subtypes.
COVID-19 impact: Recruitment was suspended during lockdowns, and follow-up delays affected 40 lesions, potentially introducing bias in final diagnosis confirmation.
Non-generalizability to other AI tools: Results apply only to this specific AIaMD (DERM); performance varies significantly among different AI-based skin cancer detection systems.

Regulatory status: The AIaMD is a Class IIa UKCA Medical Device, providing MHRA verification of safety, which supports its deployment but does not guarantee equivalent performance to other unregulated AI apps.
Real-world deployment context: The study highlights that AI performance may drop between research and real-world environments, necessitating clinical review of AI outputs in practice.
